questions.text: What are the three primitive types that an MCP server can expose?

options.label: A
options.text: Tools, Resources, and Prompts

options.label: B
options.text: Functions, Files, and Templates

options.label: C
options.text: Actions, Data, and Messages

options.label: D
options.text: Endpoints, Schemas, and Handlers

questions.explanation: MCP servers expose three primitive types: Tools (executable functions Claude can call), Resources (data sources Claude can read), and Prompts (reusable prompt templates). Each primitive serves a different purpose in the agent's context.

questions.text: When building an MCP server in Python, which class do you subclass to create a server?

options.label: A
options.text: MCPServer

options.label: B
options.text: Server from the mcp library

options.label: C
options.text: FastMCP

options.label: D
options.text: BaseServer

questions.explanation: FastMCP is the recommended high-level class from the mcp Python library for building MCP servers. It provides decorators like @mcp.tool() and @mcp.resource() that make it simple to expose functionality.

questions.text: What is the difference between an MCP Tool and an MCP Resource?

options.label: A
options.text: Tools run on the client side; Resources run on the server side

options.label: B
options.text: Tools perform actions or computations with side effects; Resources provide read-only data access

options.label: C
options.text: Tools are for text data; Resources are for binary data

options.label: D
options.text: There is no meaningful difference; they are interchangeable

questions.explanation: MCP Tools are for actions and computations that may have side effects (like sending an email or writing a file). MCP Resources are for read-only data access (like reading a file or querying a database without modifying it).

questions.text: How does an MCP server communicate with an MCP client when using the stdio transport?

options.label: A
options.text: Via a REST API over HTTP

options.label: B
options.text: Via shared memory between processes

options.label: C
options.text: Via JSON-RPC messages over standard input and standard output

options.label: D
options.text: Via WebSocket connections

questions.explanation: With the stdio transport, the MCP client launches the server as a subprocess. Communication happens via JSON-RPC 2.0 messages written to and read from the process's stdin/stdout streams.

questions.text: What must the input schema of an MCP tool define?

options.label: A
options.text: The programming language used to implement the tool

options.label: B
options.text: The expected input parameters as a JSON Schema object so Claude knows how to call the tool correctly

options.label: C
options.text: The maximum execution time allowed for the tool

options.label: D
options.text: The authentication credentials needed to invoke the tool

questions.explanation: Every MCP tool must declare its input schema as a JSON Schema object. This schema tells the MCP client (and Claude) exactly what parameters to pass, their types, and which are required — enabling Claude to call the tool with valid arguments.
